not going to go to those restaurants. That's like, okay, I got your list, now I'm going to go, make you go places you don't want to go. This is what's going to happen. So, here's the magic. The first part is the same, I don't care. Here's the second part, in this case. You know which ones I like. You pick. Magic. The only thing that changes is which one on which day. It's worked for three years. We go to exactly the same restaurants every single time, and they're the ones I like. But she picked them, and she moves them around on different days, so she picked. It's perfect. It's just perfect. If I had figured this out, this is one of the, about 10 things. If I had figured this out years earlier, I probably could have avoided a divorce. See, I used to do it the other way, and then there's a big fight, right? Well, I told you where I wanted to go. Why the hell are we going these places? Because I don't want to go where you want to go. I mean, this is, this works, okay? So, the hijack is to shove somebody into a situation where they think they're picking, whatever it is you got, but you pre-framed the pick. They still think they're picking. The other version of this is, you switch somebody from, do I want to do this or not? Am I interested in this or not? To which of these should I get? So, when I sold at all the Peter Loew events, I sold an AB offer. So, how many of you were at, how many of you, that's your origin? Those events, okay, it falls off year by year, but so for those of you who never saw them, they're these big sports arenas, 10,000, 15,000 people, Zeg, Brian Tracy, me, with celebrities in between, we're selling stuff. So, I sold an AB offer, $99, 278. In some other environments, three, but there I didn't have enough time. So, one reason was to get people to the table for the lower threshold, the 99 bucks, and then there was a switch. Switch, by the way, had to be easy, you had to be able to teach it to temps. Right, so the switch was fine, happy to have this, this will be shipped to you. Or, we have the complete package here today that you can take home with you. Would you like to upgrade, that was the switch, okay? So, that switched like half of the $99 buyers. They just immediately, they took the bag and thought they went on their merry way. But the other reason it was there is because people now would come, so they all gather, the ones that aren't buying are in a clump around me. And here's the top question. There was always some statement. I own a bakery. I just got business yesterday. I'm poor. Sydney, I had one, only one in all those years. I had one. I run an escort service. Do you know what that is? Yeah, I know what that is, I'm an adult male. So, but there was some statement about what they do. And then they would ask, which program should I get? Now, if I had only had the 278, they would have left. Never would have asked the question. Now, which program should I get? It should come as no surprise to you. That in 99% of the cases, the thing they needed was the 278. Every once in a while, over 10 years, I actually broke down. You know, it'd be like, I'm really poor and I'm sleeping in my car even I have some level of compunction. But mostly the answer was, well, you need to complete system. Okay, off they go. So, you always want to switch, should I do this or not? To which one should I do? Should I do this or not? To which one should I get? If I am selling high-priced services to a new client, I almost never propose one prescription. I almost always put two prescriptions on the table. You could do this, you could do this. And inevitably, well, which one do you think I should do? In most of the cases, you should do the big one. That's what you should do, the big ass one. Very seldom do I say, well, I think you should do it. But see, they feel like this was a collaborative process over which they had some control, right? They're no control. I picked both. Either one was acceptable to me, right? I framed the whole pick. But they feel like they picked. They go home and they say to spouse, I picked. Right? I decided, I chose. Well, he said I could do this or I could do that. I did this. Here's why I did this. They feel like they picked. And how they feel controls all sorts of things. People get sucked in by comparative choice all the time. And there's three things that affect comparative choice. These three things are not in your manual, so you might want to jot them down. One are choices of items. You're gonna get just the mightest touch system or you're gonna get mightest touch plus magnetic marketing. That's choice of item. Choice of timing. You're gonna do it now. You're gonna do it spread out over six months. Right? Differential benefit. There's two things you can do. In one, here's the benefits. In another, here's more benefits. Those are the three ways basically that we manipulate comparative choice. So here's a great instructive story. By the way, who knows what time we're supposed to be done? Five o'clock, thank you very much. It's a good year here. So when the home bread making machine was first introduced, one of those appliances nobody needs, right? When the home, I mean really. It's like, it's every once in a while somebody invites me to go fishing. I'm going, there's a supermarket. What are we living in, 1812? There's a grocery store over here. You know? So when the home bread making machine was first introduced and displayed in the Williams Sonoma stores at $270, sales were virtually non-existent. And the product was on the verge of cancellation in Williams Sonoma. When a marketing guy convinced the manufacturer to create and convinced Williams Sonoma to display a second model of the machine next to the other model. The second model was bigger, a little fancier, and priced at $400. Five by five. Sales skyrocketed. Here's what happened. When there was one machine, here was your thought. Hey, what the hell is a bread maker? All right? And do I want a bread maker? No. Here's what the big ass one next to the little one did. All right? I don't know much about bread makers, but I'd rather have the smaller one that takes less counter space. And whoop! Person instantly switched from, I don't know what a bread maker for, to I don't want that big ass one. I'll take the small one. Sales through the rub. In Neil Strauss' The Game book, I told you to read, there's a whole strategy of going hunting with a very unattractive, decoy friend. Which standing next to you makes you look better. So you immediately are going to do better if you go to the bar to pick up women, if you take a fat, ugly guy with you. And he's right there with you. By comparison, you look pretty good. By yourself, you don't look so good. It's all now comparative choice. She before said, do I really want Neil? Now she says, well, if I'm going to have one of them, I'd rather have the smaller one. It doesn't take up so much counter space. Partee, this is a strategy for you. I'm trying to help you. So when you set up comparative choice, this is what happens. So I gave you an example. Starts on your page 51. We're not going to go through it. You can go through it on your own, but it is a great comparative choice example. What's your suggestion on how to use them with finesse so that they work and you don't have problems later? Well, first of all, if they're used with finesse, the thought that there's gonna be buyers remorse is infinitely more wrong than it is right. So most buyers remorse is always, it's either really an inappropriate person being sold something in the first place. Or it is really an absence of finesse. So take your joke. So now you know, right, but you didn't know because she's good at it, right? So, but having said that, so the prevention of buyers remorse is all about what happens at and immediately after. A decision, a sale, whatever has occurred, right? And again, most people do little or nothing. So most of the places you go buy things, most of the people you go buy things from, most of the people you engage in any kind of a business or commercial relationship with, they do little or nothing to make you feel good about what just happened, right? It doesn't take much because by comparison, you are doing something that nobody else is doing. So handwritten thank you notes are like a miracle. I mean, they're like a miracle, right? And so if you just get, if you're running a professional practice and at the end of the day, handwritten notes go out for everybody that was in there before you go home. That goes a long way, but it's an overwrought concern. Most people's response to most of this is they don't know what happened to them. They don't know what happened. And so we're, you're not experiencing it here, we're describing it, right? And so obviously you and I now are both very aware of it. But when you're experiencing it, generally you don't really realize it's happening. Thanks, then. So I love the whole devil thing. Now the guy, so they take Craig's content out and they put in other content that's just a nickel short of Craig's so nobody can sue anybody. And this guy's now got these customers, right? And Craig's grumpy about it. But the time to fix it, we're fixing it, but now it's ugly. The time to fix it was in the first place where this is gonna happen. I mean, it's foreseeable. So yeah, 30 minute show is really three two minute spots with stuff around them. So you didn't see much of it and you don't see much of it in infomercials. I want to switch them from a do I want it or not to some other choice they are now making. Now to be fair, the longer you have to work with, the more practical it is to do this, right? So like I could not do the magnetic marketing thing in less than 75 minutes and I really should have 90. I need that. In order to set up a choice and explain a choice. Hi, you've talked about this a little bit. It's a question about alternate choices. I was always thinking that the right way to do it is to always have three choices, never two. And the purpose of three choices is to drive people to the item in the middle, which is the high margin one. Am I missing something? Yeah, I mean, well, you're not, necessarily missing anything. However, so like in charity, in nonprofit, five works better than three. So you will see the smart ones mostly use five, we use five checkboxes of dollar amounts. Now you're right, they're forcing them into the middle, but they're forcing them into the upper middle. Okay, so the number of choices is situational. So one thing that controls what you're gonna do is what we just talked about, it's time. So like even three choices in a limited time is more problematic than two. But in some cases, it's because of big price disparity. So two thirds of the people will usually take the better value in a binary choice. So when you dollar cost average now, what's your unit of transaction is gonna be, you're sometimes better off with two in the big spread than three evenly spaced. So there's no hard and fast rule here, and obviously in pure direct response, we would split test. When I'm selling services, and I'm now we're toward the end of a day, and we're figuring out a whole, Dan's gonna do a whole marketing system for you, and he's gonna write all this stuff, and so I would usually do two. I'm usually gonna do, here's essentially like the least we should do all at one time, and here's the Rolls Royce, here's the we do everything all at one time. I won't do three or four, and 80, 90% of the time, they're gonna take the everything. Okay? Dan, in terms of the hijacks, is the right answer as many as you can possibly?
